content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886




November 7, 2001

Senator Thomas McClintock
California State Senate
19th Senatorial District
223 E. Thousand Oaks Blvd., Suite 326
Thousand Oaks, CA 91360

Re: Information Request in re Secession Election
Our File No. G-01-227

Dear Senator McClintock:

Thank you for your September 18, 2001 request for information on behalf of your constituents regarding the upcoming secession vote in the City of Los Angeles.

Specifically, you ask whether, in the context of the upcoming secession vote, the election of the new council members is state law or Los Angeles law with regard to fundraising activities. As you know, the interplay between state and local provisions can be complicated. The answer to your question depends on many factors, including whether the election you speak of is a state or local election. Unfortunately, that answer is not governed by the Political Reform Act ("Act"). However, the Commission recently considered the complex issues surrounding the interplay between the Act's reporting requirements and secession groups. A copy of the staff memorandum prepared for the Commission is enclosed.

I hope this information is helpful to you and your constituents. If either the agency or I can provide additional information, please contact me at 916-322-5660.

Very truly yours,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
Enclosure

sections.question: Whether, in the context of the upcoming secession vote, the election of the new council members is state law or Los Angeles law with regard to fundraising activities.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether fundraising regulations for council member elections in the context of a Los Angeles secession vote are governed by state law or local law.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC indicates that the answer depends on whether the election is classified as a state or local election, which is not determined by the Political Reform Act itself, and refers the requester to an enclosed staff memorandum for further analysis of related secession reporting issues.

QUESTION: Whether, in the context of the upcoming secession vote, the election of the new council members is state law or Los Angeles law with regard to fundraising activities.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC indicates that the answer depends on whether the election is classified as a state or local election, which is not determined by the Political Reform Act itself, and refers the requester to an enclosed staff memorandum for further analysis of related secession reporting issues.
